exemplar.title: Modelling outburst of epidemy in Poland using SIR model

detailedExemplarData.title: Modelling outburst of epidemy in Poland using SIR model

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical progression with recognizable introduction, methods, results and evaluation sections
Well-organized headings, subheadings, tables and conclusion
Concise narrative that stays mostly on topic and avoids major digressions
weaknesses: Occasional typographical errors (e.g. âepidemyâ), misplaced line breaks and inconsistent table formatting
Overly long introductory paragraphs and some redundant explanations reducing conciseness
Long URLs and inconsistent reference formatting disrupt readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration demonstrates solid coherence and logical development (Band 3) with clear structure, but typographical slips and redundancies prevent it from reaching the highest band. A score of 3/4 reflects these strengths and minor lapses.

strengths: Correct use of standard notation (S, I, R, Î±, Î², dS/dt) in key equations
Inclusion of multiple representations: formulae, tables and ASCII graphs
Accurate statement of the general Euler update formula as a foundation
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (miswritten dR/dt, stray commas in numbers, unclear overlines)
ASCII-style graphs lack axes labels, scales and legends
Formatting glitches in tables and mixed narrative/equation paragraphs hinder clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language, representations and logical structure are present but only maintained at a basic level (Band 2 across all strands). A score of 2/4 reflects some appropriate use but uneven consistency and clarity.

strengths: Independent selection of Polandâs COVID-19 data and implementation of Eulerâs method
Clear personal motivation tied to a future medical career
Good creativity shown by comparing two numerical approaches
weaknesses: Approach follows standard modelling techniques without exceptional innovation
Decisions and creative steps are reasonable but not sustained at an outstanding level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows significant independent thinking and a clear personal approach with good initiative (Band 2 in all strands), but it does not reach the outstanding creativity or innovation required for the top band. Hence 2/3.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on why S, I and R curves behave as they do
Clear evaluation of model limitations and suggestions for future improvements (e.g. SEIR, SIS)
Links between parameter values and method comparison demonstrate thoughtful reflection
weaknesses: Critical analysis of results remains basic with little quantitative comparison or sensitivity analysis
Occasional conflation of concepts (mortality vs transmission rate definitions)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection goes beyond token comments and includes clear evaluation (Band 2 depth and outcomes, Band 1â2 analysis), but lacks the depth and insight for Band 3. A score of 2/3 is appropriate.

strengths: Relevant use of differential equations and Eulerâs method directly applied to the research question
Mathematics fully commensurate with the SL course level and applied purposefully
Clear demonstration of the Euler update step in a worked example
weaknesses: Conceptual errors in parameter estimation (nonstandard Î± derivation) and some sign/rate mistakes
Rounding and calculation inaccuracies (Iâ typo, misplaced commas) reduce precision
No sensitivity analysis to deepen understanding of parameter effects
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (relevance and level appropriateness) align with Band 3, and understandingâdespite errorsâshows more than minimal grasp, warranting a bump to Band 4 overall (4/6). This adjustment balances the total to the required 13 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Explanations usually precede calculations and the reader can follow the sequence of formulas.  Nonetheless, internal accuracy issues (e.g. incorrect calculation of Iâ, inconsistent sign when computing dS/dt) interrupt clarity, and some paragraphs mix narrative with equations in a confusing way.

Therefore, the logical structure is present but uneven, matching the descriptor âsome logical structure and clarityâ.

The student shows understanding of the SIR framework and Eulerâs update formulas; most computations follow the stated equations.  Nevertheless, conceptual errors (Î± derived from CFR/S, incorrect Iâ subtraction, sign mistakes, rounding anomalies) reveal gaps in accuracy.

Hence the strand fits âsome understanding with errors,â warranting band 2.

The student supplies formulae, two numerical tables and two graphs, so more than one representation is indeed present.  However, the graphs are rendered as ASCII blocks with no clear axes labels or colour distinction, and Table 3 contains anomalies that hinder interpretation.  Thus the representations are present but only partly effective.

These features correspond to the âsome use of different formsâ band rather than âeffectiveâ.

The report follows a recognisable progression (introduction â model description â calculations â results â evaluation).  Each part links logically to the next and the reader can generally follow the mathematical argument without needing to infer missing steps.

However, coherence is occasionally weakened by typographical slips (e.g. mis-spelling of âepidemyâ, misplaced line-breaks inside numbers) and by moments where the explanation of constants (Î± derived from CFR/S) is not clearly motivated, forcing the reader to pause.  These lapses prevent the work from reaching the âhighly coherent / excellentâ descriptor.

Overall, the development is solid and logical but not polished enough for the top band.

Introducing two solution techniques, constructing large data tables and attempting to justify parameter estimation demonstrate a reasonable level of initiative.  The work is not innovative enough for the top band, but it does exceed the minimal standard.

Hence a âgood level of creativity and initiativeâ is appropriate.

Analysis of results is present but basic: the student notes that S and I decrease while R increases and attributes differences between the two methods to scaling.  There is little quantitative comparison or exploration of sensitivity to Î± and Î².

Thus the work fits âbasic analysis of results.â

The evaluation section explicitly lists limitations (parameter estimation, classification of deaths, choice of start date) and suggests future directions (SEIR, SIS).  These constitute a clear evaluation with implications, consistent with band 2.

The student reflects on why the curves behave as they do and recognises that the slow growth may stem from low parameter values chosen after restrictions began.  They also discuss how assumptions (immunity, deaths included in R) limit the model.

These comments go beyond a token mention and constitute meaningful reflection, but are not probing enough for âsubstantial critical reflection.â

Standard notation (S, I, R, Î±, Î², dS/dt) is used correctly most of the time and equations are typeset.  Nevertheless, there are frequent slips: dR/dt is written with a lowercase âlâ, numbers contain stray commas and spaces, and some subscripts are handled inconsistently.

Because appropriate language appears but is not maintained âmostly consistentlyâ, the work aligns best with the âsome appropriate mathematical language and notationâ level.

The exploration is clearly framed around the studentâs personal interest in medicine and the Polish context.  Method selection (Euler versus direct difference equations) reflects a deliberate, personal route through the topic rather than following a template.

This aligns with "clear evidence of a personal approach".

The vast majority of text is relevant to modelling COVID-19 with SIR, yet the write-up includes superfluous rounding justifications, repeated statements that S and I decrease while R increases, and long URL strings inside the body.  These additions reduce conciseness.

Still, outright digressions are limited and the essential narrative remains clear, so the work is judged âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ rather than âminimal coherenceâ.

Differential equations, numerical iteration and Eulerâs method are all relevant mathematics for HL AA, and they are applied directly to the research question.  Although parameter estimation is shaky, the selected mathematics is appropriate and purposeful, matching the descriptor ârelevant mathematics used appropriately.â

The student independently selects Polandâs COVID-19 data, estimates parameters, and implements Eulerâs method in Excel.  These choices go beyond merely reproducing a textbook example and show personal decision-making.

While the mathematics is standard, the personal link to a future medical career and the data-driven parameter choices constitute âsignificant evidence of independent thinking.â

Headings, sub-headings, numbered tables and a concluding section demonstrate clear organisation.  The reader can quickly locate definitions, assumptions and results.  Despite some formatting glitches in Table 3 (misaligned digits and symbols) the underlying structure remains evident and functional.

The exploration contains an introduction, method, two parallel solution approaches, visualisations, conclusion, evaluation and references â a full set of structural components expected at HL.

Minor presentation flaws (ASCII-style graphs, occasional table corruption) keep the organisation from the exceptional level, but it still merits the âwell organised with clear structureâ band.

The modelling uses first-order ODEs and a numerical method, both firmly within HL course content and applied in a genuine context.  The work therefore meets, but does not surpass, the expectation that mathematics be "commensurate with course level."

annotations.comment: The title contains a spelling error âepidemyâ; it should be âepidemic.â Correcting this will improve professionalism and clarity.
This introductory paragraph is overly long and contains informal phrasing. Consider dividing it into shorter paragraphs and focusing on conciseness to maintain reader engagement.
The notation â\overline{a}â in the list item is unclear and not previously defined. Replace or define this symbol consistently in your mathematical notation.
Table 1âs caption is too generic. Enhance it to explain exactly how S, I and R relate dynamically in the model to guide the reader.
The initial condition description lacks the specific calendar date for t = 0. Specify for clarity (e.g., ât = 0 corresponds to 1 September 2020â).
The equation is miswritten as dR/dt = Î²l (lowercase âlâ). It should read $$\frac{dR}{dt}=\beta I$$ to be consistent with the model.
The description of Î± as âproportional to mortalityâ is conceptually incorrect; Î± should represent transmission rate per contact. Clarify its epidemiological meaning.
The explanation of dS/dt duplicates earlier assumptions. Consider condensing to avoid redundancy and keep focus on new information.
The formula $$\alpha=\frac{CFR}{S}$$ is nonstandard and lacks epidemiological justification. Provide rationale or revise according to standard SIR parameter estimation.
The narrative conflates mortality rate and transmission rate when defining Î±. Separate the definitions of CFR and transmission constant for clarity.
The Iâ calculation has a typo â67,00922â instead of â67,922.â Correct the number formatting to avoid reader confusion.
When substituting into $$S_0=N-(I_0+R_0)$$, include units (number of individuals) to reinforce interpretation.
Rounding CFR to 0.019 then Î± to two decimal places amplifies error. Maintain higher precision in intermediate steps for accuracy.
The worked example for $$S_{t+1}$$ is clear and well presented, effectively demonstrating the Euler update step.
The Excel formula table in Table 2 is misaligned and lacks clear labels. Reformat to align columns and provide descriptive headers.
The large data table (Table 6) contains formatting inconsistencies (spaces in numbers, stray overlines). Clean up number formatting for readability.
The general Euler formula $$y_{n+1}=y_n+h\,f(x_n,y_n)$$ is stated accurately and sets a clear foundation.
The normalization to proportions is mathematically correct, but you should explain why modeling in percentages is advantageous in this context.
In the expression for Sâ the closing parenthesis is missing around the product, causing potential confusion. Ensure parentheses are balanced.
Table 4 in Excel duplicates earlier formulas without adding new insight. Consider summarizing once or providing key results instead of repeating.
The ASCII table used as a graph lacks axes labels, scales and legend. Replace with a properly plotted chart to enhance interpretation.
Your comparison of the two methods links parameter values to trends, demonstrating meaningful reflection on results.
The evaluation restates known assumptions but lacks quantitative sensitivity analysis on Î± and Î². Including this would deepen critical reflection.
When comparing to another work, you mention [11] without context. Provide an in-text citation detail (e.g., author and year) to anchor this comparison.
Long URLs in the reference entries disrupt readability. Use DOIs or shorten URLs and move full links to an appendix or footnotes.
The reference formatting is inconsistent (missing dates or access statements). Ensure each entry follows a uniform citation style.
